feed_card.headline: Most mammals get about 1 billion heartbeats in a lifetime, whether they live 2 years or 70.

card_visual.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 56 32' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><path d='M2 16 H18 L22 16 L26 6 L32 26 L37 16 H54' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='3' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/></svg>

teasers: Why shrews and whales share one number
The math that predicts your heart rate
How humans broke the billion-beat pattern

sections.content: Most mammals get about 1 billion heartbeats in a lifetime, whether they live 2 years or 70.

content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><rect x='58' y='16' width='284' height='32' rx='16' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2' fill='var(--accent)' fill-opacity='0.10'/><text x='200' y='37' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='15' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>all reach ~1 billion lifetime beats</text><line x1='40' y1='240' x2='372' y2='240' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.4' stroke-linecap='round'/><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.55'><rect x='49' y='90' width='52' height='150' rx='6'/><rect x='134' y='108' width='52' height='132' rx='6'/><rect x='219' y='163' width='52' height='77' rx='6'/><rect x='304' y='215' width='52' height='25' rx='6'/></g><g fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'><text x='75' y='82'>1,000 bpm</text><text x='160' y='100'>600 bpm</text><text x='245' y='155'>130 bpm</text><text x='330' y='207'>30 bpm</text></g><g fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' text-anchor='middle'><text x='75' y='262'>Shrew</text><text x='160' y='262'>Mouse</text><text x='245' y='262'>Cat</text><text x='330' y='262'>Elephant</text></g><g fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.65' text-anchor='middle'><text x='75' y='281'>lives 2 yr</text><text x='160' y='281'>3 yr</text><text x='245' y='281'>15 yr</text><text x='330' y='281'>65 yr</text></g></svg>

sections.content: Measured in years, lifespans look random. A shrew gets 2, a cat 15, an elephant 65. Now count the heartbeats those years contain. They come out almost the same, near a billion in every case. So the year, the unit we trust, hides what is going on underneath. A small animal is not short on time so much as quick to spend it. The shrew packs a week of our heart-work into a single day.

items: At one beat every second without stopping, a billion beats would take 31 years and 8 months.
A typical adult passes their billionth heartbeat at around age 27.
An elephant spends its whole billion over about 63 years, while a mouse burns through it in 3.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 33 400 184' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='52' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='middle'>One human life</text><rect x='40' y='140' width='320' height='22' rx='11' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.4'/><rect x='40' y='140' width='102' height='22' rx='11' fill='var(--accent)' opacity='0.22'/><line x1='142' y1='116' x2='142' y2='186' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round'/><text x='142' y='108' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='15' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>1 billionth beat</text><text x='142' y='204' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.7' text-anchor='middle'>age 27</text><text x='251' y='132' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.6' text-anchor='middle'>beyond the budget</text><text x='42' y='204' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.6' text-anchor='start'>birth</text><text x='358' y='204' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.6' text-anchor='end'>~80 yrs</text></svg>

sections.content: There is no clever measurement behind this. You take an animal's resting pulse, you take how long it lives, you multiply. People have been writing both numbers down for over a century, from zoo logs to field studies. The catch is that you cannot see it in any single animal. A mouse is just a mouse with a fast heart and a short life. It shows up only when you line up species of wildly different sizes and watch their totals come to rest in the same place.

content.body: In 1883 the German physiologist Max Rubner offered a tidy reason big animals burn energy slowly: they shed heat through their skin. Metabolism should then track surface area and scale as mass to the 2/3 power. The logic was clean and it held the field for 50 years. Then in 1932 Max Kleiber, a Swiss chemist measuring oxygen use in animals from rats to oxen at a California agricultural station, found his data would not sit on Rubner's line. Metabolism scaled as mass to the 3/4 power. Heart rate ran as its mirror image, falling as mass to the negative 1/4 power. Doubling an animal's weight drops its pulse by about 16%. Why 3/4 and not 2/3, nobody could say, and the exponent went unexplained for 65 years. In 1997 the physicist Geoffrey West, working with biologists James Brown and Brian Enquist, pulled the number out of the body's plumbing. Blood vessels branch like a fractal tree that fills the body and ends in capillaries of one fixed size. That single constraint forces the 3/4 law. The same year the cardiologist Herbert Levine multiplied heart rate by lifespan across 15 species, watched the products gather near a billion, and gave the pattern its name.

key_figures.name: Max Kleiber
key_figures.one_line: In 1932 his measurements showed animal metabolism scaling as the 3/4 power of body mass.

key_figures.name: Geoffrey West
key_figures.one_line: Trained as a physicist, he showed in 1997 that the 3/4 exponent falls straight out of the fractal way blood vessels branch.

key_figures.name: Herbert J. Levine
key_figures.one_line: The cardiologist who brought the billion-beat budget into medicine, plotting heart rate against lifespan across 15 species in 1997.

content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round' opacity='0.5'><line x1='60' y1='240' x2='370' y2='240'/><line x1='60' y1='240' x2='60' y2='40'/></g><line x1='80' y1='220' x2='350' y2='60' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round'/><g fill='currentColor'><circle cx='95' cy='213' r='3.5'/><circle cx='135' cy='185' r='3.5'/><circle cx='175' cy='167' r='3.5'/><circle cx='210' cy='140' r='3.5'/><circle cx='245' cy='125' r='3.5'/><circle cx='285' cy='97' r='3.5'/><circle cx='325' cy='77' r='3.5'/></g><g fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.7'><text x='215' y='270' text-anchor='middle'>Body mass (log)</text><text x='22' y='140' text-anchor='middle' transform='rotate(-90 22 140)'>Metabolic rate (log)</text></g><text x='350' y='52' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='15' font-weight='700' text-anchor='end'>slope = 3/4</text></svg>

content.title: Modern humans live past the biological budget
content.body: A human heart at 70 beats a minute over 80 years runs up about three billion beats, three times what most mammals get. The hearts themselves are nothing special. At 70 bpm we sit right where the formula puts an animal our size. What moved was lifespan. Before antibiotics, clean water, and safe childbirth, people typically died somewhere near 30 or 40, squarely on the universal billion. Those extra beats came from the decades modern survival added past the old budget.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round' opacity='0.5'><line x1='60' y1='250' x2='370' y2='250'/><line x1='60' y1='250' x2='60' y2='40'/></g><line x1='70' y1='185' x2='360' y2='185' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5' opacity='0.4' stroke-dasharray='6 6'/><text x='70' y='178' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.65' text-anchor='start'>~1B budget</text><g fill='currentColor' opacity='0.85'><circle cx='110' cy='188' r='3.5'/><circle cx='175' cy='183' r='3.5'/><circle cx='245' cy='186' r='3.5'/><circle cx='320' cy='182' r='3.5'/></g><circle cx='210' cy='95' r='5.5' fill='var(--accent)'/><text x='210' y='82' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='15' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>Humans ~3B</text><g fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.7'><text x='215' y='278' text-anchor='middle'>Mammals by size</text><text x='22' y='150' text-anchor='middle' transform='rotate(-90 22 150)'>Lifetime beats</text></g></svg>

content.title: Other primates beat the budget too, with no medicine to help
content.body: Humans are not the only primates over the line. A 2023 study in Montpellier put the gray mouse lemur at about three billion lifetime beats. This 60-gram primate lives a dozen years, three times what a rodent its size would spend. That high count, the study found, is what sets primates apart from other mammals their size, whatever the body weight. The advantage looks built into primate biology, not borrowed from medicine, though why has not been pinned down.
content.image_caption: The gray mouse lemur, a 60-gram primate that runs up roughly three billion lifetime beats.
content.image_attribution: Charles J. Sharp, “Gray Mouse Lemur 1”, CC BY-SA 3.0 (Wikimedia Commons)

content.title: Inside a single species, the rule turns over
content.body: Across species, bigger means longer-lived. Within a species it reverses. A Great Dane is old at 7 or 8. A Chihuahua often passes 15 while weighing 30 times less. The same shows up in lab mice, where the larger strains die younger and extra growth hormone shortens life. The billion-beat rule describes the differences evolution built between species.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='44' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='middle'>Within one species</text><line x1='60' y1='250' x2='340' y2='250' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.4' stroke-linecap='round'/><rect x='110' y='177' width='60' height='73' rx='12' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.5'/><text x='140' y='168' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>Great Dane</text><text x='140' y='219' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.8' text-anchor='middle'>7-8 yr</text><text x='140' y='270' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.6' text-anchor='middle'>~30 kg</text><rect x='230' y='95' width='60' height='155' rx='12' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='260' y='86' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>Chihuahua</text><text x='260' y='178' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>15+ yr</text><text x='260' y='270' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.6' text-anchor='middle'>~2 kg</text></svg>

sections.content: So the real variable in a lifespan is the pace of spending. If a body spends a roughly fixed budget of beats, how long it lives comes down mostly to how fast it spends. Every gain humans have made in lifespan bought more years at the same pace. Underneath, the rate of wear has not changed.

content.question: What stays roughly constant across mammals, from shrew to whale?
options: Total heartbeats over a lifetime
Resting heart rate
Lifespan in years
Heart size relative to the body
content.explanation: Heart rate and lifespan each vary enormously, but their product, the total beats in a life, lands near a billion for most mammals. Small animals spend the budget fast, large ones slowly.

content.question: Why do a mouse and an elephant end up near the same lifetime beat count?
options: They share a recent common ancestor with the trait
Their hearts are the same size relative to body mass
Evolution fixed lifespan and heart rate separately at matching values
A faster heart goes with a shorter life, and the two effects cancel
content.explanation: Body mass drives heart rate down and lifespan up at related rates set by the same scaling laws. Multiply the two and the size effect cancels, leaving a near-constant product.

content.question: Kleiber's 1932 data showed metabolism scales with body mass raised to which power?
options: 1, so it scales linearly with mass
It does not depend on mass
content.explanation: Surface-area reasoning had predicted 2/3. Kleiber's measurements landed on 3/4, and the same exponent later turned up in plants and microbes, hinting at a deeper cause.

content.question: When a mammal's body mass doubles, its resting heart rate roughly...
options: doubles
drops by about 16%
halves
stays the same
content.explanation: Heart rate falls as mass to the negative 1/4 power. 2 to the negative 1/4 is about 0.84, so doubling the mass shaves roughly 16% off the rate.

content.question: Modern humans run up about three billion beats, three times the mammal budget. Why?
options: Our heart muscle uses a tougher protein than other mammals
Our resting heart rate is unusually slow for our size
Evolution selected our hearts for extra durability
Medicine and hygiene let us live well past the lifespan biology selected for
content.explanation: At about 70 bpm our hearts sit right where the formula puts an animal our size. What changed is survival: we no longer die young from infection, childbirth, or injury, so the meter keeps running.

content.question: Inside a single species, how does body size relate to lifespan?
options: Larger individuals tend to live shorter lives, reversing the cross-species pattern
It matches the cross-species pattern, so larger individuals live longer
There is no relationship between size and lifespan within a species
Only birth weight matters, not adult size
content.explanation: Great Danes die years before Chihuahuas, and larger mouse strains die younger than small ones. Within a species, bigger bodies tend to die sooner, the reverse of the cross-species trend.

content.question: What did West, Brown, and Enquist propose in 1997 to explain the 3/4 exponent?
options: Heat loss through skin surface sets the rate
The branching geometry of blood vessels constrains the scaling
Random variation across many species averages to 3/4
Cellular metabolism is intrinsically fractal
content.explanation: They modeled the circulatory system as a space-filling fractal network of branching tubes ending in fixed-size capillaries. That geometric constraint forces the 3/4 exponent, and the model has since been applied to plants and even cities.

content.myth: A faster heart rate means a less efficient cardiovascular system.
content.reality: A small body needs a fast heart. A shrew's thousand-beat pulse is what the physics of its size demands, and its heart is no less efficient than an elephant's.

content.myth: All mammals get exactly one billion heartbeats.
content.reality: Across non-human mammals the figure runs from roughly half a billion to two billion, and some primates clear a billion easily. One billion is the order of magnitude.

content.myth: Modern humans evolved more durable hearts than our ancestors.
content.reality: Heart muscle and resting rate are no different from those of prehistoric humans, and the same hardware now runs for more years.

content.body: The pattern is solid as a statistical regularity, but why it holds is not settled. Heart rate and lifespan both scale with body mass. But the mechanism that ties a life to a rough beat budget is still argued over. Metabolic rate, cellular wear, and oxidative damage have all been proposed, and none fully closes the case. The clearest clue is the exceptions. They are what the open questions are about.
items: Why do some species, naked mole rats and certain primates among them, run far more lifetime beats than their size predicts?
How much of the human figure is biology, and how much is medicine and safety extending lives the body was not built for?
Does one mechanism explain the relationship, or do several unrelated ones happen to line up?

content.label: Levine HJ. Rest heart rate and life expectancy. JACC 1997;30(4):1104-1106
West GB, Brown JH, Enquist BJ. A general model for the origin of allometric scaling laws in biology. Science 1997;276:122-126

DiFrancesco ML et al. Characterization of sinoatrial automaticity in Microcebus murinus to study the effect of aging on cardiac activity and the correlation with longevity. Scientific Reports 2023;13:3054
